name: Phase 3 DMD trial found tadalafil did not slow ambulatory decline
statement: In 331 boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, once-daily tadalafil for 48 weeks did not lessen decline in 6-minute walk distance or secondary ambulatory outcomes versus placebo, despite hypothesis-generating upper-limb signals in boys older than 10 years.
population_or_context: Glucocorticoid-treated boys age 7 to 14 years with DMD.
quantitative_note: 6MWD declined 51.0 +/- 9.3 m with placebo, 64.7 +/- 9.8 m with low-dose tadalafil (P=0.307 vs placebo), and 59.1 +/- 9.4 m with high-dose tadalafil (P=0.538 vs placebo).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Abstract reports trial size, doses, primary and secondary outcomes, PUL exploratory signal, safety, and classification of evidence.

population_profile: Pediatric boys with DMD; very low directness for healthy men.
endpoint_measure: 6-minute walk distance, North Star Ambulatory Assessment, timed function tests, Performance of Upper Limb.
effect_estimate: No significant benefit on 6MWD or secondary ambulatory outcomes.
safety_findings: Adverse events were consistent with known tadalafil safety profile and DMD disease state.
directness_to_target_population: Very low for healthy adult men, but important boundary against muscle-perfusion overreach.
confounding_and_bias_limits: Pediatric rare disease, glucocorticoid background, disease progression, and no healthy-user performance endpoint.
class_transfer_limits: Direct tadalafil evidence, but DMD context does not transfer to healthy gym performance.
